claim.statement: Engineering teams should gate all new production features behind feature flags rather than deploying them to all users simultaneously

grounds.content: Teams using feature flags reduce mean time to recovery (MTTR) by 60% on average compared to teams doing big-bang releases (LaunchDarkly State of Feature Management 2023)
grounds.source: LaunchDarkly State of Feature Management Report, 2023

grounds.content: Amazon, Netflix, and Google have all publicly documented progressive rollout as a core deployment practice that reduces blast radius of defects
A feature that is deployed but dark can be activated for 1% of users, then 10%, then 100% — allowing observation at each stage without a separate staging environment

warrants.statement: Practices that reduce MTTR and blast radius of defects should be adopted by teams that value production reliability
Incremental rollout is a form of empirical validation — each stage is evidence before full commitment

backings.content: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) literature (Beyer et al., Google SRE Book) establishes MTTR and blast-radius reduction as canonical reliability objectives
The empirical cycle (hypothesis → test → observe → expand) is foundational to controlled experiment design; feature flags operationalize this cycle in production

conditions: Team has a feature flag management system (not just ad hoc if/else checks)
Flags are cleaned up after full rollout — flag debt is managed
Feature is observable (metrics exist to evaluate each rollout stage)

rebuttals.objection: Feature flags add code complexity — every flag is a branch, and stale flags become technical debt

response.content: Valid: flag debt is real and teams must enforce flag lifecycle discipline (expiry dates, automated staleness warnings). The qualifier already requires a flag management system. The complexity cost is real but lower than the cost of a full-traffic incident.

rebuttals.objection: Small teams with low traffic may not benefit — they can monitor a full release in hours anyway

response.content: Correct for very low-traffic applications where a full release is observable within one business hour. The policy should be scoped to teams with meaningful traffic volumes or high incident cost.

keyInsight: The non-obvious point: feature flags shift the risk model from binary (it works or it doesn't, at full blast) to graduated (observe failure signals at 1% before they become incidents at 100%). The warrant — that reliability teams should minimize blast radius — is the load-bearing premise; teams that dispute this are disputing a foundational SRE principle, not just the feature-flag tactic.
